ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for raw pig tongue as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for pork organ meats as food items is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw pig tongue is approximately 100 g, equivalent to a small whole tongue or a thick cross-sectional slice before cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, fresh, variety meats and by-products, tongue, raw — FDC ID 171700
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry FDC ID 171700, 'Pork, fresh, variety meats and by-products, tongue, raw'. This is an approved primary source providing comprehensive macronutrient, fatty acid, micronutrient, and mineral data for raw pig tongue. HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F06090021) core macros (energy 225 kcal, protein 16.3 g, fat 17.2 g, sodium 110 mg, potassium 243 mg, calcium 16 mg, iron 3.35 mg, cholesterol 101 mg) are consistent with USDA FDC values and have been retained; USDA FDC provides the additional fatty acid fractions and vitamin data.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — food contains negligible carbohydrates
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database — not applicable for zero-carbohydrate foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pig tongue, raw, contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g (confirmed per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171700). Glycemic index is not applicable for foods with negligible carbohydrates. The University of Sydney GI Database does not assign GI values to zero-carbohydrate foods. glycemic_index is set to null (not 0) per Veritas scoring rules for negligible-carbohydrate foods. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 by definition. gi_category is set to 'not_applicable'.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (110 mg/100 g) and moderate potassium (243 mg/100 g) in raw form
hypertension.guidance: Raw pig tongue has a low sodium content of 110 mg/100 g and a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio; however, patients with hypertension should avoid high-sodium cooking methods such as braising in soy sauce or curing, and should moderate portion size given the saturated fat content (~6 g/100 g) which may contribute to arterial stiffness.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar; no glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pig tongue, raw, contains 0 g carbohydrate and 0 g sugar per 100 g, producing no glycaemic response; it is a suitable high-protein source (16.3 g/100 g) for individuals with type 2 diabetes, but saturated fat (~6 g/100 g) should be monitored as chronic high saturated fat intake may worsen insulin resistance, and sweet braising sauces or glazes that add sugar should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~6 g/100 g), cholesterol (101 mg/100 g), and trace trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pig tongue, raw, contains approximately 6 g saturated fat and 101 mg cholesterol per 100 g, both of which can raise LDL cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 50–75 g per occasion, choose lean cooking methods such as steaming or boiling, and avoid high-fat accompaniments or cured preparations.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat (~6 g/100 g exceeding the 5 g/100 g RA threshold), trace trans fat, and arachidonic acid from pork fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pig tongue is not recommended for individuals with active rheumatoid arthritis due to its saturated fat content exceeding the 5 g/100 g RA threshold, trace trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g), and arachidonic acid from pork fat, all of which promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; if consumed, pair with anti-inflammatory herbs such as ginger or turmeric and limit to small portions outside flare periods.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pig tongue, raw, is an uncooked porcine organ meat sourced from the tongue muscle of the domestic pig (Sus scrofa domesticus). In its raw state it displays a pale pink to deep pink colour with a firm, dense, and slightly gelatinous texture owing to its high collagen and muscle fibre content. The tongue is covered by a thick papillated mucosa that is typically removed by blanching or peeling before consumption. Raw pig tongue is a nutrient-dense food providing 225 kcal, 16.3 g protein, and 17.2 g fat per 100 g, with zero carbohydrate and zero sugar, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. It is a meaningful source of iron (3.35 mg/100 g), potassium (243 mg/100 g), and calcium (16 mg/100 g). Cholesterol is moderate at 101 mg/100 g and saturated fat is approximately 6 g/100 g. Pig tongue is consumed across many culinary traditions including Chinese braised dishes (lu wei), European charcuterie, Latin American lengua tacos, and Southeast Asian offal preparations. It is commonly braised, stewed, steamed, or pickled after initial blanching. Clinically, it is appropriate for type 2 diabetes management due to its zero glycaemic impact, but saturated fat and cholesterol content warrant moderation for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis patients. Sodium in raw form is low at 110 mg/100 g, though cooking methods such as soy-sauce braising substantially increase sodium load.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171700), an approved primary source. HPB Singapore Foodo core macro values are consistent with USDA FDC. All values are internally consistent and comprehensive.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171700 for raw pig tongue — this is naturally zero as raw pork organ meat contains no digestible carbohydrate. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is an animal-derived organ meat containing no plant fibre. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100 g); gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to null per Veritas rules for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Halal status is marked false as pig products are not halal; kosher status is false as pork is not kosher. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03311349 and FoodEx2 code A0CKP are best-match defensible codes; SNOMED CT has no identified code for raw pig tongue as a food item. vitamin_a_mcg_rae and vitamin_d_mcg are reported as 0.0 (confirmed trace/negligible per USDA FDC ID 171700).

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to null (not 0) as food contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g — GI is not applicable for negligible-carbohydrate foods per Veritas rules. gi_category set to 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. gi_source references the University of Sydney GI Database with explicit documentation that GI is not applicable for zero-carbohydrate foods. This satisfies the q4 rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods.
